explanation: This LTL formula captures the requirement that whenever the pilot is in control (standby is true), eventually the autopilot will transition from the TRANSITION state to the STANDBY state.  The formula  `G(standby -> F(state_is_STANDBY & !state_is_TRANSITION))`  ensures that whenever 'standby' is true, it will eventually be the case that the system is in the STANDBY state and not in the TRANSITION state.

This LTL formula captures the requirement that whenever the autopilot is in the TRANSITION state (state_is_TRANSITION) and the pilot is in control (standby), it will eventually transition to the STANDBY state (state_is_STANDBY).  It ensures that the autopilot will change to standby mode when the pilot is in control, but does not specify that it should only do so when the pilot is in control.  Therefore, it might be possible for the autopilot to transition to STANDBY in other scenarios, which may not be desired.

The LTL property (standby -> (state_is_TRANSITION -> X(state_is_STANDBY))) captures the natural language by stating that if the pilot is in control (standby), then whenever the autopilot is in the TRANSITION state, it will transition to the STANDBY state in the next time step.  This captures the requirement that the autopilot should change states from TRANSITION to STANDBY when the pilot is in control.

This LTL formula captures the requirement that whenever the autopilot is in the TRANSITION state and the pilot is in control (standby), the autopilot will eventually transition to the STANDBY state. The formula uses the 'until' operator (U) to express this temporal relationship. It asserts that whenever the state_is_TRANSITION is true and standby is true, it will eventually become true that state_is_STANDBY is true.

This assumes that the autopilot will only transition from TRANSITION to STANDBY when the pilot is in control, which is a reasonable assumption given the provided context.

It uses the "G" (always) operator to specify that this condition must hold at all times, and the "->" (implies) operator to connect the antecedent (pilot in control and autopilot in "TRANSITION" state) to the consequent (eventually transitioning to "STANDBY" state).  The "F" (eventually) operator is used to express that the transition to "STANDBY" state will happen at some point in the future.
